revision.comment: v2.05b - WPCleaner - Fix errors for [[WP:WCW|CW project]] (DEFAULTSORT missing for titles with special letters)
1 revision imported from [[:wikipedia:Ryūtōsan_Shrine]]: importing single revision from english wikipedia to provide context instead of writing an inferior article for here
Bot: Migrating 1 langlinks, now provided by [[d:|Wikidata]] on [[d:q11180716]]
1 revision imported: reimporting the Kokuhei Shosha from complex English wikipedia in case simple ones overwrote them accidentally
Copying from [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] to [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|1678–1945 Shinto shrine in Korea}} {{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ryutosan Shrine circa 1930.JPG | coordinates = {{coord|35.1008|129.0325|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ōmononushi]]
([[Konpira Gongen]])
[[Kunitama|Kunitama Okami]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Okami]] | consecration_year = 1678 | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}Location relative to modern-day Busan }} {{Nihongo|'''Ryūtōsan Shrine'''|龍頭山神社}} was a [[Shinto shrine]] in Korea. It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|1678–1945 Shinto shrine in Korea}} {{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ryutosan Shrine circa 1930.JPG | coordinates = {{coord|35.1008|129.0325|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ōmononushi]]
([[Konpira Gongen]])
[[Kunitama|Kunitama Okami]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Okami]] | consecration_year = 1678 | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}Location relative to modern-day Busan }} {{Nihongo|'''Ryūtōsan Shrine'''|龍頭山神社}} was a [[Shinto shrine]] in Korea. It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]]

Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]]

Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]]

Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia44]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]]

It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kotohira shrines]] {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]]

{{Short description|1678–1945 Shinto shrine in Korea}} {{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ryutosan Shrine circa 1930.JPG | coordinates = {{coord|35.1008|129.0325|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ōmononushi]]
([[Konpira Gongen]])
[[Kunitama|Kunitama Okami]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Okami]] | consecration_year = 1678 | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to modern-day Busan }} {{Nihongo|'''Ryūtōsan Shrine'''|龍頭山神社}} was a [[Shinto shrine]] in Korea. It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ryūtōsan It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. {{Short description|1678–1945 Shinto shrine in Korea}} {{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ryutosan Shrine circa 1930.JPG | coordinates = {{coord|35.1008|129.0325|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ōmononushi]]
([[Konpira Gongen]])
[[Kunitama|Kunitama Okami]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Okami]] | consecration_year = 1678 | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to modern-day Busan }} {{Nihongo|'''Ryūtōsan Shrine'''|龍頭山神社}} was a [[Shinto shrine]] in Korea. It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[category:Shinto Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] revision.text:
{{Short description|1678–1945 Shinto shrine in Korea}} {{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ryutosan Shrine circa 1930.JPG | coordinates = {{coord|35.1008|129.0325|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ōmononushi]]
([[Konpira Gongen]])
[[Kunitama|Kunitama Okami]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Okami]] | consecration_year = 1678 | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to modern-day Busan }} {{Nihongo|'''Ryūtōsan Shrine'''|龍頭山神社}} was a [[Shinto shrine]] in Korea. It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] {{Short description|1678–1945 Shinto shrine in Korea}} {{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ryutosan Shrine circa 1930.JPG | coordinates = {{coord|35.1008|129.0325|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ōmononushi]]
([[Konpira Gongen]])
[[Kunitama|Kunitama Okami]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Okami]] | consecration_year = 1678 | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to modern-day Busan }} {{Nihongo|'''Ryūtōsan Shrine'''|龍頭山神社}} was a [[Shinto shrine]] in Korea. It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrines in the {{Short description|1678–1945 Shinto shrine in Korea}} {{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ryutosan Shrine circa 1930.JPG | coordinates = {{coord|35.1008|129.0325|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ōmononushi]]
([[Konpira Gongen]])
[[Kunitama|Kunitama Okami]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Okami]] | consecration_year = 1678 | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to modern-day Busan }} {{Nihongo|'''Ryūtōsan Shrine'''|龍頭山神社}} was a [[Shinto shrine]] in Korea. It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrines in the revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|1678–1945 Shinto shrine in Korea}} {{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ryutosan Shrine circa 1930.JPG | coordinates = {{coord|35.1008|129.0325|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ōmononushi]]
([[Konpira Gongen]])
[[Kunitama|Kunitama Okami]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Okami]] | consecration_year = 1678 | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to modern-day Busan }} {{Nihongo|'''Ryūtōsan Shrine'''|龍頭山神社}} was a [[Shinto shrine]] in Korea. It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira Shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ryūtōsan Its former site is now part of [[Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Ryūtōsan Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Ryūtōsan Shrine]] [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ryutosan Shrine}} [[Category:New]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[ja:龍頭山神社]][[ko:용두산신사]][[simple:Ryūtōzan Shrine]][[zh:龍頭山神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11180716]] Wikidata name: Ryūtōsan Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|1678–1945 Shinto shrine in Korea}} {{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Ryutosan Shrine circa 1930.JPG | coordinates = {{Coord|35.1008|N|129.0325|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ōmononushi]]
([[Konpira Gongen]])
[[Kunitama|Kunitama Okami]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Okami]] | consecration_year = 1678 | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to modern-day Busan | prefecture= {{ill|Busan|WD=Q16520}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Ryūtōsan Shrine'''|龍頭山神社}} was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in Korea. It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally Ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[:en:Yongdusan Park|Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ryūtōsan Shrine}} [[ja:龍頭山神社]] [[ko:용두산신사]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Ryūtōzan Shrine]] [[zh:龍頭山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11180716}}

Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally Ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[:en:Yongdusan Park|Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] }} [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ryūtōsan Shrine}} [[ja:龍頭山神社]] [[ko:용두산신사]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Ryūtōzan Shrine]] [[zh:龍頭山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11180716}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally Ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of [[:en:Yongdusan Park|Yongdusan Park]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]] {{Commons category|Ryūtōsan Shrine}} [[ja:龍頭山神社]] [[ko:용두산신사]] [[simple:Ryūtōzan Shrine]] [[zh:龍頭山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11180716}} [[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[co:commons:Category:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[ja:龍頭山神社]][[ko:용두산신사]][[simple:Ryūtōzan Shrine]][[zh:龍頭山神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[ja:龍頭山神社]][[ko:용두산신사]][[simple:Ryūtōzan Shrine]][[zh:龍頭山神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]][[ja:龍頭山神社]][[ko:용두산신사]][[simple:Ryūtōzan Shrine]][[zh:龍頭山神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11180716]] Wikidata name: Ryūtōsan Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|1678–1945 Shinto shrine in Korea}} {{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Ryutosan Shrine circa 1930.JPG | coordinates = {{Coord|35.1008|N|129.0325|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = [[Ōmononushi]]
([[Konpira Gongen]])
[[Kunitama|Kunitama Okami]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Okami]] | consecration_year = 1678 | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to modern-day Busan | prefecture= {{ill|Busan|WD=Q16520}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Ryūtōsan Shrine'''|龍頭山神社}} was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in Korea. It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally Ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of {{ill|Yongdusan Park|en|Yongdusan Park|WD=Q483056|lt=Yongdusan Park}}. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]] {{Commons category|Ryūtōsan Shrine}} [[ja:龍頭山神社]] [[ko:용두산신사]] [[simple:Ryūtōzan Shrine]] [[zh:龍頭山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11180716}} [[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

([[Konpira Gongen]])
[[Kunitama|Kunitama Okami]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Okami]] | consecration_year = 1678 | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to modern-day Busan | prefecture= {{ill|Busan|WD=Q16520}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Ryūtōsan Shrine'''|龍頭山神社}} was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in Korea. It is the earliest shinto shrine in Korea{{Cite book |last=Shimizu |first=Karli |url=https://www.amazon.com/Overseas-Shinto-Shrines-Secularity-Bloomsbury/dp/1350234982 |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}{{rp|139}} built by workers of the local Japan House trade office in 1678. Such shinto may have served a purpose of expressing and maintaining a Japanese identity outside of Japan, while the Japanese and Korean people lived relatively close during this period.{{Cite journal |last=Grisafi |first=John G. |title=Shintō in Colonial Korea: A Broadening Narrative of Imperial Era Shintō |url=https://www.academia.edu/28557377}} It was a [[Kotohira shrines|Kotohira shrine]] dedicated to [[Ōmononushi]] and the protection of sailors.{{cite journal |last1=Nakajima |first1=Michio |year=2010 |title=Shinto Deities That Crossed the Sea: Japan's" Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945. |url=http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/3002 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |access-date=31 January 2016}} In addition it, alongside all other nationally Ranked shinto shrines in Korea enshrined [[Amaterasu]] and [[Kunitama]] as a pair.{{rp|139}} It was originally called {{Nihongo||金刀比羅神社|Kotohira shrine}}, before being renamed {{Nihongo|Ryūtōsan Shrine|龍頭山神社|Ryūtōsan Jinja}}. It was destroyed after the liberation of Korea. Its former site is now part of {{ill|Yongdusan Park|en|Yongdusan Park|WD=Q483056|lt=Yongdusan Park}}. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]] {{Commons category|Ryūtōsan Shrine}} [[ja:龍頭山神社]] [[ko:용두산신사]] [[simple:Ryūtōzan Shrine]] [[zh:龍頭山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11180716}} [[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Its former site is now part of {{ill|Yongdusan Park|en|Yongdusan Park|WD=Q483056|lt=Yongdusan Park|1=Yongdusan Park}}. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Korea-reli-stub}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{Kotohira Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[en:Ryūtōsan Shrine]] {{Commons category|Ryūtōsan Shrine}} [[ja:龍頭山神社]] [[ko:용두산신사]] [[simple:Ryūtōzan Shrine]] [[zh:龍頭山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11180716}} [[Category:1678 establishments]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in South Korea]] [[Category:History of Busan]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]
